The surgeon performs a laparoscopic-assisted right hemicolectomy, beginning with the mobilization of the terminal ileum by dissecting the peritoneum. A medial approach is used to visualize the surgical trunk on the superior mesenteric vein. The iliocolic vein and right colic vein are divided, followed by a functional end-to-end anastomosis through a small upper median incision. The procedure transitions to a cholecystectomy, where the cystic duct is double-clipped and divided.

Using the sonazoid view, the surgeon visualizes two liver metastases located in segment five. Intraoperative sonography confirms the tumor locations and their relationship to major blood vessels. The surgeon begins laparoscopic segment V subsegmentectomy by mobilizing the right side of the liver to minimize blood loss during parenchymal transection. A laparoscopic harmonic scalpel is used to open the country line and locate the right anterior Glissonian pedicle, which is double-clipped and divided using a tissue sealing device.

The surgeon continues the parenchymal dissection using KUSA, visualizing additional branches of the glistoneal pedicle in segment five. These branches are clipped and divided with Enseal. The dissection proceeds along the demarcation line using a harmonic scalpel. A peripheral branch of the middle hepatic vein from segment five is identified and resected. The anatomical resection of segment five is completed, ensuring both metastases are included in the resected specimen with a safe surgical margin.

The surgeon performs superficial liver parenchymal dissection using a harmonic scalpel and deeper dissection with KUSA or Enseal. The dissected segment five is placed into a sterile bag and retrieved through a five-centimeter upper median incision made for the colectomy anastomosis. The video shows the simultaneous laparoscopic resection of colorectal cancer and synchronous liver metastasis.

We have accumulated sixteen patients from two thousand two until two thousand ten. Nine were female. Nine and seven were originated from colon. Liver resection was partial in eight. Segmentectomy two, sectionectomy two, and lobectomy four. Pure laparoscopic procedure was twelve cases. Laparoscopic assisted procedure was four cases. Median operation time was three hundred sixty minutes, median blood loss was one hundred sixty three, and median hospital stay was two weeks. In conclusion, simultaneous laparoscopic resection of colorectal cancer and synchronous liver metastasis can be safely and feasibly performed at our institute. Thank you for your attention.
